title: Keep Food Safe to Eat
summary: Learn the basics of handling food so it stays safe to eat and does not make you sick.
learnerPromise: Keep food safe to eat by handling it correctly.
whyItMatters: Food that sits out too long or touches dirty surfaces can grow germs that make you sick. Small habits like washing hands and checking dates go a long way toward keeping meals safe.
realLifeUses: Check if leftovers are still good before eating them
Know when to throw out expired items
Keep raw meat separate from ready-to-eat foods
youWillLearn: How to read date labels on food
When to wash your hands while cooking
miniChallenge: Check your fridge for one item past its date. Throw it out and wipe the spot.
steps: Wash your hands with soap and warm water for 20 seconds.
Look through your fridge and find the date printed on at least three items.
Identify one item that is past its date and set it aside to throw away.
Wipe the empty shelf or spot with a clean cloth or paper towel.
Wash your hands again after handling old food.
completionCriteria: At least one expired item is removed from the fridge
Hands are washed before and after handling food
commonProblems: Not sure if a date is a sell-by or use-by date — check the label wording carefully
Food looks and smells fine but the date passed — when in doubt, throw it out
Forgot to wash hands between handling raw and ready-to-eat foods
tips: Keep a marker near the fridge to write dates on leftovers
Store raw meat on the bottom shelf so juices do not drip onto other foods
Set a phone reminder to check dates once a week
helpBoundary: When in doubt, throw it out. Ask a food safety professional if you are unsure about a specific food.
